_meta.note: Salary figures are illustrative U.S. national medians from the BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook (May 2024). They are starting points for conversation, NOT guarantees. Verify current local numbers via each career's BLS link. Each career includes a step-by-step roadmap from high school to landing the job.

careers.name: Software Developer

paths.label: B.S. Computer Science
paths.note: Traditional route. Internships matter more than GPA.

paths.label: Coding bootcamp
paths.note: Faster and cheaper, but you must build a portfolio.

paths.label: Self-taught + projects
paths.note: Hardest path to a first job, but real for the disciplined.

careers.forage: Try the JPMorgan Software Engineering or Wells Fargo simulations on Forage (free).

mentor.name: Priya R.
mentor.quote: Nobody tells you 70% of the job is reading other people's code and sitting in meetings.
day: 9:00 — Stand-up meeting, say what I'm working on (2 min).
9:30 — Code. Mostly fixing a bug a customer reported.
12:30 — Lunch, usually at my desk reading docs.
1:30 — Code review: read a teammate's work, leave comments.
mentor.loved: Building something and watching thousands use it the next day.
mentor.hard: Imposter syndrome is real. Tech changes constantly — you never stop learning.
mentor.advice: Build one tiny app this month. You'll learn more than a semester of theory.

roadmap.step: Crush math; take any CS class; build one small app or website.
Choose a route: a CS degree, a coding bootcamp, or disciplined self-study.
Learn one language deeply and ship 2–3 real projects to GitHub.
Land an internship or freelance gig; contribute to open source.
Polish your portfolio, apply widely, and practice coding interviews.

careers.name: Registered Nurse

paths.label: BSN (Bachelor's)
paths.note: Preferred by hospitals; opens more doors.

paths.label: ADN (Associate)
paths.note: Faster, cheaper way in; bridge to BSN later while working.

careers.forage: Search Forage for healthcare and life-sciences experiences.

mentor.name: Marcus T.
mentor.quote: It is physically and emotionally hard, and also the most meaningful work I can imagine.
day: 6:45 — Get report from the night shift.
7:30 — Med pass, check vitals, chart everything.
10:00 — A trauma comes in. Everything else waits.
1:00 — Eat half a sandwich standing up.
7:15 — Hand off to next shift. 12-hour day done.
mentor.loved: A patient hugged me and said I made the scariest day of their life bearable.
mentor.hard: 12-hour shifts on your feet, nights and weekends, and you see people on their worst days.
mentor.advice: Volunteer or get a CNA job in high school. You'll know within a month if it's for you.

roadmap.step: Take biology and chemistry; volunteer or get a CNA job.
Enroll in a BSN (4 yr) or ADN (2 yr) nursing program.
Master clinical skills and pass your nursing courses and labs.
Complete supervised clinical rotations in hospitals during school.
Pass the NCLEX-RN exam and earn your state license.

careers.name: Electrician

paths.label: Paid apprenticeship
paths.note: You get PAID to train. No degree required.

paths.label: Trade school + apprenticeship
paths.note: Short program first, then apprentice hours.

careers.forage: Trades aren't on Forage — book a real job-shadow with a local contractor instead.

mentor.name: Dee W.
mentor.quote: I have zero student debt and own a house. My friends with degrees are still paying theirs off.
day: 6:30 — Load the van, drive to the job site.
7:00 — Pull wire and set boxes for a new building.
10:00 — Troubleshoot a circuit that won't pass inspection.
12:00 — Lunch from the cooler with the crew.
3:30 — Clean up, plan tomorrow, head home.
mentor.loved: I can point at buildings and say 'I wired that.' And the pay is solid with no debt.
mentor.hard: Hot attics, cold crawlspaces, early mornings. Your body is your tool — protect it.
mentor.advice: Call three local electrical contractors and ask to shadow for a day. Most say yes.

roadmap.step: Take shop, math, and physics; find summer helper or labor work.
Apply to a paid apprenticeship (IBEW or a local contractor).
Attend trade classes and learn the National Electrical Code.
Log 8,000+ supervised hours working under a journeyman.
Pass the licensing exam to become a journeyman electrician.

careers.name: Mechanical Engineer

paths.label: B.S. Mechanical Engineering
paths.note: Required. Heavy math and physics — be honest about that.

careers.forage: Try engineering simulations on Forage from companies like GE or Lockheed.

mentor.name: Sofia L.
mentor.quote: Real engineering is 80% spreadsheets and emails, 20% the cool design stuff from class.
day: 8:30 — Check email, review a contractor's questions.
9:30 — Design work in CAD, size equipment for a building.
11:30 — Site visit to check an install.
5:00 — Wrap up the report, log off.
mentor.loved: I designed a system that keeps a whole hospital comfortable. That's real impact.
mentor.hard: The degree is genuinely hard. Many start engineering and switch out freshman year.
mentor.advice: If calculus excites you more than it scares you, this could be your path.

roadmap.step: Excel in calculus and physics; join a robotics or maker club.
Start a B.S. in Mechanical Engineering at an accredited school.
Master CAD, thermodynamics, and the core engineering courses.
Do co-ops or internships every summer you can.
Graduate, pass the FE exam, and apply for engineering roles.

careers.name: UX / Web Designer

paths.label: Bachelor's (design/HCI)
paths.note: Helps, but a strong portfolio beats a degree here.

paths.label: Bootcamp/certificate + portfolio
paths.note: Very viable. Employers hire on portfolio, not diploma.

careers.forage: Search Forage for design and product experiences.

mentor.name: Jordan K.
mentor.quote: Your portfolio is your degree. I've hired people with no college and turned down master's grads.
day: 9:00 — Review user feedback on a feature.
10:00 — Sketch and design screens in Figma.
12:00 — Present designs to engineers and PMs.
3:00 — User-testing call, watch someone use the product.
4:30 — Document decisions, hand off to engineering.
mentor.loved: Watching a confused user suddenly 'get it' because of something I designed.
mentor.hard: Everyone has an opinion on design, so you defend your choices constantly.
mentor.advice: Redesign an app you hate. Put it online. That's the start of a portfolio.

roadmap.step: Take art/design classes; redesign an app you dislike for fun.
Choose a route: a design degree or a UX bootcamp/certificate.
Learn Figma and user research; build three case studies.
Do a design internship or freelance for a real client.
Apply with a strong portfolio — it matters more than the degree.

careers.name: Physician

paths.label: Bachelor's → Med School → Residency
paths.note: Long, expensive, intense. High pay comes much later.

careers.forage: Shadow a doctor and volunteer in a hospital before committing to this path.

mentor.name: Dr. Amir H.
mentor.quote: I love it now, but I gave up my whole 20s and finished training with $280k in debt.
day: 1:00 — More patients, plus calls and refills.
5:00 — Charting that didn't get done during the day.
mentor.loved: I've delivered babies and caught cancers early. The trust patients give you is sacred.
mentor.hard: The path is 11+ years and huge debt. Burnout is common. Be sure before you start.
mentor.advice: Shadow a doctor for a week. Volunteer in an ER. Love the reality, not the TV version.

roadmap.step: Ace biology and chemistry; volunteer at a hospital and shadow a doctor.
Finish a bachelor's with pre-med courses; score well on the MCAT.
Complete four years of medical school (MD or DO).
Match into a 3–7 year residency in your chosen specialty.
Pass your board exams and get licensed to practice.

careers.name: Welder
careers.outlook: Steady; high demand for skilled welders

paths.label: Trade school / certificate
paths.note: Short and cheap. Pipe & underwater welders earn far more.

paths.label: Apprenticeship
paths.note: On-the-job training, little to no debt.

careers.forage: Not on Forage — visit a welding school open house or shadow a fab shop.

mentor.name: Ray P.
mentor.quote: Certified pipe and underwater welders can clear six figures. The ceiling is higher than people think.
day: 6:00 — Gear up, review the day's blueprints.
6:30 — Set up the rig and start laying beads.
10:00 — Inspector checks my welds with X-ray.
3:30 — Clean station, log work, head out.
mentor.loved: Specialty welding pays incredibly well and I'm never bored. Every job is different.
mentor.hard: Sparks, heat, and you must protect your eyes and lungs. Travel can be heavy.
mentor.advice: Take a welding class at a community college. You'll know fast if you have the hands for it.

roadmap.step: Take a welding or metal-shop class; practice safe technique.
Enroll in a short welding certificate program or apprenticeship.
Master MIG, TIG, and stick welding, and learn to read blueprints.
Earn AWS certifications; practice pipe or structural welding.
Get hired — specialize (pipe, underwater) to boost your pay.

careers.name: Accountant

paths.label: B.S. Accounting + CPA
paths.note: CPA license boosts pay and options significantly.

careers.forage: Try the PwC, KPMG, or Deloitte simulations on Forage (free).

mentor.name: Hannah B.
mentor.quote: Stable, well-paid, in demand everywhere — but 'busy season' means 60-hour weeks.
day: 11:00 — Call with a client about their books.
mentor.loved: Every business needs this, so I have huge job security and can work anywhere.
mentor.hard: Busy season is brutal. It's detail work — small errors matter a lot.
mentor.advice: Do a Forage accounting simulation this weekend. It mirrors the real entry-level work.

roadmap.step: Take math and any business class; get comfortable in Excel.
Start a B.S. in Accounting at a four-year school.
Master financial accounting, tax, and audit coursework.
Do a Forage sim and a summer internship at a firm.
Graduate, pass the CPA exam, and join a firm or company.

careers.name: Data Scientist

paths.label: B.S. in stats, CS, or math
paths.note: Most common route; many add a master's later.

paths.label: Bootcamp + project portfolio
paths.note: Works if you can show real analysis on real data.

careers.forage: Try data and analytics simulations on Forage.

mentor.name: Wei C.
mentor.quote: Most of the job is cleaning messy data, not building fancy AI models.
day: 9:00 — Pull and clean a new dataset.
11:00 — Build a model and test whether it actually helps.
3:30 — Document the analysis and next steps.
mentor.loved: Finding a pattern in data that changes a real business decision.
mentor.hard: Roughly 80% of the work is unglamorous data cleaning.
mentor.advice: Analyze a dataset you care about — sports, games, music — and share what you find.

roadmap.step: Build strong math and stats; learn to code in Python.
Pursue a degree in stats, CS, or math (or a serious bootcamp).
Master statistics, SQL, and machine-learning basics.
Analyze real datasets and publish projects to a portfolio.
Apply for analyst/data roles; many add a master's to stand out.

careers.name: Cybersecurity Analyst

paths.label: B.S. in cybersecurity or IT
paths.note: Common, but certifications often matter more than the degree.

paths.label: Certifications (Security+, etc.)
paths.note: Many start in IT support, then certify their way in.

careers.forage: Try cybersecurity simulations (Mastercard, Datacom) on Forage.

mentor.name: Tariq M.
mentor.quote: It's part detective work, part teaching everyone else not to click bad links.
day: 1:00 — Run a phishing test on staff.
3:00 — Patch systems and write up findings.
mentor.loved: Stopping a real attack before it does any damage.
mentor.hard: The threats never stop, and you're on call when something breaks.
mentor.advice: Set up a home lab and learn the free TryHackMe or picoCTF challenges.

roadmap.step: Tinker with computers and networks; learn security basics.
Get an IT or cybersecurity degree, or start in IT support.
Earn certifications like Security+ and practice on TryHackMe.
Work a help-desk or junior security role to gain experience.
Move into a security analyst role and keep certifying.

careers.name: IT Support Specialist

paths.label: Certifications (CompTIA A+)
paths.note: A fast, cheap on-ramp into tech with no degree required.

paths.label: Associate degree
paths.note: Helps, and can bridge toward higher IT roles.

careers.forage: Search Forage for IT and tech support experiences.

mentor.name: Brianna O.
mentor.quote: This is the best foot-in-the-door job in tech — half of security and dev folks started here.
day: 10:30 — Set up laptops for new hires.
1:00 — Walk someone through a fix over the phone.
mentor.loved: Fixing something in two minutes that had someone stuck all morning.
mentor.hard: People only call when they're already frustrated.
mentor.advice: Get your CompTIA A+ — it's cheap and employers recognize it instantly.

roadmap.step: Build, fix, and tinker with PCs; help friends and family.
Study for the CompTIA A+ certification (no degree required).
Learn operating systems, networking, and troubleshooting.
Land a help-desk or junior IT role to build a track record.
Get hired full-time; use it as a launchpad into security or dev.

careers.name: Dental Hygienist

paths.label: Associate degree in dental hygiene
paths.note: Strong pay for two years of school, plus a state license.

careers.forage: Shadow a hygienist at a local dental office to see the real day.

mentor.name: Lacey N.
mentor.quote: Great pay, flexible schedule, and I can work three or four days a week.
day: 8:00 — Review the day's patients and X-rays.
9:00 — Clean teeth and check for problems.
1:00 — More patients, plus coaching on home care.
mentor.loved: Strong pay and a flexible schedule with only a two-year degree.
mentor.hard: It's repetitive and physically hard on your hands and neck.
mentor.advice: Tour a dental hygiene program and shadow for a day before applying.

roadmap.step: Take biology and chemistry; shadow a hygienist at a clinic.
Enroll in an accredited dental-hygiene associate program (2–3 yr).
Master cleaning, X-rays, and patient-care coursework.
Complete supervised clinical hours during the program.
Pass national and state board exams to get licensed.

careers.name: Physical Therapist

paths.label: Bachelor's → Doctor of Physical Therapy
paths.note: A doctorate (DPT) is required — long and expensive, but stable pay.

careers.forage: Volunteer or shadow in a PT clinic before committing to the doctorate.

mentor.name: Greg A.
mentor.quote: I help people walk again after surgery — but the schooling is a full seven years.
day: 8:00 — First patient recovering from knee surgery.
10:00 — Design exercise plans and track progress.
mentor.loved: Watching a patient take their first steps again after an injury.
mentor.hard: The doctorate is long and pricey, and insurance paperwork is endless.
mentor.advice: Shadow a PT for a week — it's required for most programs anyway.

roadmap.step: Take biology and anatomy; volunteer in a PT clinic.
Earn a bachelor's with the prerequisite science courses.
Complete a 3-year Doctor of Physical Therapy (DPT) program.
Do supervised clinical rotations with real patients.
Pass the national licensure exam (NPTE) to practice.

careers.name: Pharmacist

paths.label: Pre-pharmacy → Doctor of Pharmacy (PharmD)
paths.note: A doctorate and license are required; high pay but heavy debt.

careers.forage: Shadow a retail or hospital pharmacist to see the difference.

mentor.name: Nadia F.
mentor.quote: The pay is excellent, but you stand all day and accuracy is life-or-death.
mentor.loved: Catching a dangerous drug interaction before it reaches a patient.
mentor.hard: Long days on your feet, and zero room for error.
mentor.advice: Get a pharmacy-tech job in high school — it confirms the fit and helps applications.

roadmap.step: Excel in chemistry and biology; work or shadow at a pharmacy.
Finish pre-pharmacy courses (2–4 yr) and apply to PharmD school.
Complete a four-year Doctor of Pharmacy (PharmD) program.
Do pharmacy rotations in retail and hospital settings.
Pass the NAPLEX licensing exam and get hired.

careers.name: Paramedic / EMT

paths.label: EMT certificate
paths.note: Very fast, cheap entry; start saving lives within a year.

paths.label: Paramedic program
paths.note: More training and higher pay than basic EMT.

careers.forage: Trades like this aren't on Forage — ride along with a local EMS crew.

mentor.name: Cole R.
mentor.quote: You get into this for the moments where minutes truly decide everything.
day: 12:00 — Restock between calls, grab food when you can.
mentor.loved: Being the calm in someone's worst moment, and sometimes saving a life.
mentor.hard: Hard shifts, tough scenes, and modest pay for the stress.
mentor.advice: Get your EMT cert first — it's fast and tells you if the work is for you.

roadmap.step: Take biology; get CPR-certified; look into a cadet/Explorer post.
Complete an EMT certificate (a few months).
Gain field experience as an EMT, then train as a paramedic.
Finish paramedic school and your clinical ride-along hours.
Pass the NREMT exam and get hired by an EMS agency.

careers.name: Veterinarian

paths.label: Bachelor's → Doctor of Veterinary Medicine
paths.note: As competitive as med school, with similar debt.

careers.forage: Volunteer at a clinic or shelter — it's required for vet-school applications.

mentor.name: Priscilla D.
mentor.quote: I love the animals; the hard part is the owners and the emotional toll.
day: 11:00 — See sick pets and give vaccines.
mentor.loved: Sending a sick pet home healthy to a happy family.
mentor.hard: Euthanasia days are brutal, and vet-school debt is heavy.
mentor.advice: Volunteer at a shelter or clinic now — you need the hours and the reality check.

roadmap.step: Ace science classes; volunteer at a shelter or animal clinic.
Earn a bachelor's with pre-vet science courses.
Get into and complete four years of veterinary school (DVM).
Do clinical rotations and internships working with animals.
Pass the licensing exam (NAVLE) and start practicing.

careers.name: Plumber

paths.label: Paid apprenticeship
paths.note: Get paid to train; no degree needed. Owners can earn a lot.

careers.forage: Not on Forage — shadow a local plumbing company for a day.

mentor.name: Sal V.
mentor.quote: People will always need plumbers, the work can't be outsourced, and I own my business.
day: 7:00 — Drive to the first service call.
mentor.loved: Recession-proof work, no debt, and I run my own crew now.
mentor.hard: Dirty jobs, tight spaces, and the occasional 2 a.m. emergency.
mentor.advice: Apply to a union apprenticeship — they pay you to learn the trade.

roadmap.step: Take shop and math; find a helper job with a plumber.
Apply to a paid plumbing apprenticeship (4–5 yr).
Attend trade classes; learn codes, fixtures, and pipefitting.
Log supervised on-the-job hours under a master plumber.
Pass the licensing exam to become a journeyman plumber.

careers.name: HVAC Technician

paths.label: Trade school + apprenticeship
paths.note: Short, affordable training and steady year-round demand.

careers.forage: Not on Forage — visit an HVAC trade-school open house.

mentor.name: Manny G.
mentor.quote: Everyone needs heating and cooling, so I'm busy in every season.
day: 7:30 — Load the van, head to the first call.
8:30 — Service an A/C unit before summer.
11:30 — Diagnose a furnace that won't fire.
2:00 — Install a new thermostat and system.
mentor.loved: Steady demand, good pay, and no two days the same.
mentor.hard: Hot attics in summer, cold roofs in winter.
mentor.advice: A short trade-school program plus EPA certification gets you working fast.

roadmap.step: Take shop, math, and physics; tinker with mechanical things.
Enroll in a short HVAC trade program (6 mo–2 yr).
Learn refrigeration, electrical, and airflow systems.
Apprentice with a company and earn EPA certification.
Get hired as an HVAC technician; specialize to earn more.

careers.name: Carpenter

paths.label: Paid apprenticeship
paths.note: Learn on the job; specialize later for higher pay.

careers.forage: Not on Forage — ask a local builder or contractor to shadow.

mentor.name: Owen P.
mentor.quote: I make things that last for decades, and I never sit at a desk.
mentor.loved: Pointing at finished homes and knowing I built them.
mentor.hard: Physically demanding, weather-dependent, and hard on the body over time.
mentor.advice: Try a high-school shop class or a weekend build — you'll know fast if you love it.

roadmap.step: Take wood shop and math; build small projects at home.
Join a paid carpentry apprenticeship (3–4 yr).
Learn framing, finishing, blueprints, and tool safety.
Work supervised hours on real job sites.
Become a journeyman; specialize (finish, framing) for higher pay.

careers.name: Automotive Technician

paths.label: Trade school + ASE certification
paths.note: EV and hybrid specialists are in growing demand.

careers.forage: Not on Forage — visit a dealership or shop and ask to observe.

mentor.name: Hector M.
mentor.quote: Modern cars are computers on wheels — diagnostics is the real skill now.
day: 8:00 — Run diagnostics on the day's cars.
mentor.loved: Solving a problem three other shops couldn't figure out.
mentor.hard: You often buy your own tools, and it's hard on your back and hands.
mentor.advice: Get ASE certified and specialize in EVs — that's where the money is going.

roadmap.step: Take auto shop; fix bikes, cars, and small engines for practice.
Enroll in a trade-school auto program (6 mo–2 yr).
Learn diagnostics, engines, brakes, and electrical systems.
Apprentice at a shop or dealership; earn ASE certifications.
Get hired; specialize in EVs/hybrids for the best pay.

careers.name: Financial Analyst

paths.label: B.S. in finance, economics, or accounting
paths.note: A CFA credential boosts pay and options.

careers.forage: Try the JPMorgan, Citi, or BofA finance simulations on Forage (free).

mentor.name: Devon S.
mentor.quote: It's storytelling with numbers — the spreadsheet is only half the job.
day: 1:00 — Build a slide deck for leadership.
mentor.loved: My analysis directly shaping a multi-million-dollar decision.
mentor.hard: Long hours, and the markets don't care about your weekend plans.
mentor.advice: Do a Forage finance simulation — it's the exact entry-level work.

roadmap.step: Take math and economics; learn Excel and follow the markets.
Earn a B.S. in finance, economics, or accounting.
Master financial modeling, accounting, and valuation.
Do a Forage finance sim and a summer internship.
Graduate, apply for analyst roles, and pursue the CFA to advance.

careers.name: Marketing Manager

paths.label: Bachelor's + experience
paths.note: You start as a coordinator and grow into management.

careers.forage: Try marketing and brand simulations on Forage.

mentor.name: Ines B.
mentor.quote: Half art, half spreadsheet — you need both the idea and the numbers to back it.
mentor.loved: Watching a campaign I built take off and drive real sales.
mentor.hard: You're judged on numbers that the market partly controls.
mentor.advice: Start a social account or blog and grow it — that's a real portfolio.

roadmap.step: Run a social account or blog; take any business/media class.
Earn a bachelor's in marketing, communications, or business.
Learn analytics, content, SEO, and brand strategy.
Intern, then start as a coordinator or specialist.
Deliver results and get promoted into management.

careers.name: Project Manager

paths.label: Bachelor's + PMP certification
paths.note: Many fall into PM from another role; the PMP cert formalizes it.

careers.forage: Search Forage for project and operations simulations.

mentor.name: Renee K.
mentor.quote: My job is to remove obstacles so everyone else can do their best work.
mentor.loved: Shipping a big project on time with a team that trusts each other.
mentor.hard: You own the outcome but rarely control all the pieces.
mentor.advice: Lead a club, event, or group project — that's project management in miniature.

roadmap.step: Lead a club, team, or event to practice organizing people.
Earn a bachelor's in business or your industry of interest.
Learn planning tools, budgeting, and Agile basics.
Start in a coordinator role and own small projects.
Earn the PMP certification and step into PM roles.

careers.name: Civil Engineer

paths.label: B.S. in civil engineering + PE license
paths.note: A Professional Engineer (PE) license raises pay and responsibility.

careers.forage: Try infrastructure and engineering simulations on Forage.

mentor.name: Marco T.
mentor.quote: I design the roads and bridges thousands of people use without thinking about it.
mentor.loved: Driving over a bridge and knowing I helped design it.
mentor.hard: The degree is math-heavy, and public-safety stakes are high.
mentor.advice: If you like physics and building things, this is a stable, respected path.

roadmap.step: Excel in math and physics; join a build or robotics club.
Start a B.S. in Civil Engineering (ABET-accredited).
Master structures, materials, and design coursework.
Do internships with firms or public agencies.
Graduate, pass the FE exam, then earn your PE license.

careers.name: Electrical Engineer

paths.label: B.S. in electrical engineering
paths.note: One of the harder degrees, but in demand across many industries.

careers.forage: Try engineering simulations from tech and energy firms on Forage.

mentor.name: Yuki S.
mentor.quote: From phones to power grids, almost every modern thing has an EE behind it.
day: 1:30 — Debug why a board isn't working.
mentor.loved: Holding a working device that started as a sketch on my notepad.
mentor.hard: The coursework is genuinely tough — calculus and physics heavy.
mentor.advice: Build with an Arduino or Raspberry Pi kit to test your interest cheaply.

roadmap.step: Crush calculus and physics; build with Arduino kits.
Start a B.S. in Electrical Engineering.
Master circuits, signals, and electromagnetics.
Do co-ops or internships in industry.
Graduate, pass the FE exam, and apply for EE roles.

careers.name: Architect

paths.label: Professional degree + internship + license
paths.note: Requires an accredited degree, paid internship hours, and the ARE exam.

careers.forage: Shadow at an architecture firm and build a sketch portfolio.

mentor.name: Lena H.
mentor.quote: It's the meeting point of art, engineering, and a whole lot of building codes.
day: 11:00 — Client meeting on a new building.
mentor.loved: Walking into a finished building I imagined years earlier.
mentor.hard: Long path to licensure, and early-career pay is modest.
mentor.advice: Take art and drafting classes and build a portfolio of sketches and models.

roadmap.step: Take art, drafting, and math; build a sketch/model portfolio.
Enroll in an accredited architecture degree (B.Arch or M.Arch).
Master design studios, building codes, and CAD/BIM tools.
Complete paid internship hours (AXP) at a firm.
Pass the ARE exams to become a licensed architect.

careers.name: High School Teacher

paths.label: Bachelor's + teaching license
paths.note: Public-school teaching requires a state license.

careers.forage: Volunteer to tutor or assist a classroom to test the fit.

mentor.name: Mr. Ellis
mentor.quote: The summers and the kids are the reward; the pay and the paperwork are the trade-off.
day: 7:30 — Prep and set up the classroom.
12:30 — Grade and plan during your one free period.
mentor.loved: A former student coming back to say I changed their path.
mentor.hard: Pay is modest and the workload spills into evenings.
mentor.advice: Tutor or assist a teacher now — it's the truest test of whether you'll love it.

roadmap.step: Tutor classmates; volunteer or assist in a classroom.
Earn a bachelor's in your subject or in education.
Complete a teacher-prep program and student teaching.
Student-teach in a real classroom under a mentor.
Pass your state's exams and earn your teaching license.

careers.name: Police Officer

paths.label: Police academy (HS diploma; some require college)
paths.note: Many departments pay you to attend the academy.

careers.forage: Not on Forage — ask your local department about ride-along programs.

mentor.name: Officer Reyes
mentor.quote: Most of the job is talking to people and solving problems, not what you see on TV.
mentor.loved: Helping someone on the worst day of their life.
mentor.hard: Shift work, real danger, and intense public scrutiny.
mentor.advice: Do a ride-along and look into Police Explorer programs for teens.

roadmap.step: Stay fit and out of trouble; join a Police Explorer program.
Meet department requirements (HS diploma; some need college).
Apply and pass the written, physical, and background checks.
Complete the police academy (4–7 months).
Get sworn in and start as a patrol officer with field training.

careers.name: Firefighter

paths.label: Fire academy + EMT certification
paths.note: Most firefighters are also certified EMTs.

careers.forage: Not on Forage — look into volunteer fire departments and cadet programs.

mentor.name: Captain Doyle
mentor.quote: The brotherhood and the calls keep you in; the sleep schedule tests you.
day: 1:00 — Medical and fire calls as they come.
Evening — Cook with the crew; sleep at the station.
mentor.loved: The team feels like family, and the work genuinely matters.
mentor.hard: 24-hour shifts, real danger, and hard things you can't unsee.
mentor.advice: Get EMT-certified and volunteer — it's the standard way in.

roadmap.step: Stay very fit; volunteer with a fire department if you can.
Get EMT-certified and take fire-science courses.
Complete a fire academy and physical-ability training.
Volunteer or intern to build hands-on experience.
Pass testing and get hired by a fire department.

careers.name: Lawyer

paths.label: Bachelor's → Law School (JD) → Bar exam
paths.note: Three years of law school plus the bar; debt can be heavy.

careers.forage: Try the legal simulations from firms on Forage.

mentor.name: Aisha R.
mentor.quote: It's far more reading and writing than dramatic courtroom speeches.
mentor.loved: Winning a case that genuinely changed a client's life.
mentor.hard: Long hours, heavy debt, and a competitive job market.
mentor.advice: Join debate, and intern at a law office to see the real (paperwork-heavy) day.

roadmap.step: Join debate; sharpen reading and writing; keep grades high.
Earn any bachelor's degree, then score well on the LSAT.
Complete three years of law school (earn your JD).
Intern at law firms or clerk during school.
Pass the bar exam in your state and get licensed.

careers.name: Graphic Designer

paths.label: Bachelor's in design
paths.note: Helps, but your portfolio matters far more.

paths.label: Self-taught + portfolio
paths.note: Very viable — clients and employers hire on your work.

careers.forage: Search Forage for design and creative experiences.

mentor.name: Theo M.
mentor.quote: Nobody asks for your diploma — they ask to see your portfolio.
day: 3:30 — Prepare final files for print or web.
mentor.loved: Seeing my design on a product, poster, or screen in the real world.
mentor.hard: Lots of subjective feedback, and freelancing income can be uneven.
mentor.advice: Redesign logos or posters you dislike and post them — that's your portfolio.

roadmap.step: Take art classes; learn Photoshop/Illustrator; design for fun.
Choose a route: a design degree or self-taught + portfolio.
Master typography, color, and tools like Adobe and Figma.
Freelance or intern to get real client work.
Apply with a strong portfolio — that's what gets you hired.

careers.name: Chef

paths.label: Work up from the kitchen
paths.note: Many top chefs never went to culinary school.

paths.label: Culinary school
paths.note: Optional; faster path to leadership roles for some.

careers.forage: Not on Forage — get a kitchen job; it's the real training.

mentor.name: Chef Bianca
mentor.quote: It's hot, fast, and exhausting — and I wouldn't trade the rush for anything.
day: 3:00 — Plan the menu and order supplies.
mentor.loved: The energy of a packed dinner service that goes perfectly.
mentor.hard: Nights, weekends, holidays, low early pay, and serious heat.
mentor.advice: Get a job in any kitchen — that's where you learn if you can take the heat.

roadmap.step: Cook constantly; get any kitchen job, even dishwashing.
Decide: work up through kitchens or attend culinary school.
Learn knife skills, stations, food safety, and speed.
Work the line and move up: prep, line cook, sous chef.
Earn a head-chef role by proving leadership and consistency.
